results.possibleIdentity: Howard Alstad (often spelled Alstad or potentially Austad) was a Canadian artist, art director, and illustrator based in Toronto. He was professionally active from the late 1960s through the 1990s. In the early 1970s, he was recognized as a contributing artist to the UNICEF Greeting Card Fund, with his name appearing in a 1973 United Nations financial report under a list of artists who granted reproduction rights for their work. This period corresponds with a well-documented collaboration between Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and UNICEF (specifically the "Children and Communication" project and the greeting card program from 1969–1971), which sought to involve contemporary artists in international humanitarian causes. Alstad's broader creative career included work as a creative director at major Canadian advertising agencies such as MacLaren McCann and Alstad/Garneau, as well as designing and illustrating album covers, such as for Hugo Strasser in 1976. In the 1990s, he transitioned into directing television commercials.

Howard Jones (1922–1991) was an American artist who gained prominence in the 1960s for his experimental works that integrated technology, light, and sound. He was an active participant in projects associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. One of his most notable works from this era is 'Solo Two' (1966), a life-size silhouette of the artist programmed with light and sound sequences, which was a direct product of the art-and-technology movement championed by E.A.T. and the Howard Wise Gallery in New York. Jones's work focused on the relationship between human experience and man-made tools in the electronic age, making him a central figure in the E.A.T.-affiliated community during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Kenneth (Ken) Marsh is a pioneer video artist and media activist who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is most notably recognized as a co-founder of the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City around 1969/1970 and later as the director of Woodstock Community Video (WCV) in Woodstock, New York. His work was central to the early 'guerrilla television' and community video movement, focusing on the use of portable video technology (such as the Sony Portapak) to facilitate social dialogue and provide an alternative to corporate broadcast television. Marsh is the author of the influential book 'Independent Video: A Complete Guide to the Physics, Operation, and Application of the New Television,' published in 1974 by Straight Arrow Books. He was also a contributor to the seminal video art publication 'Radical Software' and is historically linked to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization through his participation in the emerging art-and-technology community of the time. His projects often involved environmental video performances and public access cable experiments aimed at 'activating the information flow' within communities.
